did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI and machine learning techniques for classifying skin lesions. Specifically, it extracted shallow features (position-wise color and SIFT) and deep features using the MobileNetV3 transfer learning model (pre-trained on ImageNet). These features were fused and used to train and tune five traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes) for binary classification of malignant vs. benign skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel method for classifying skin lesions by fusing shallow and deep features. It details a specific methodology including dataset acquisition (HAM10000), image preprocessing, feature extraction (SIFT, MobileNetV3), and classification using traditional machine learning models. It reports original results, including specific accuracy metrics (81% for SVM), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Fusion of Shallow and Deep Features for Classifying Skin Lesions
journal: 2022 25th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying malignant versus benign skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images. The proposed method fuses shallow features, specifically position-wise color and Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT), with deep features extracted via the MobileNetV3 transfer learning model. These combined features were used to train and tune five traditional machine learning classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Decision Tree, and Naive Bayes. The SVM model achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 81% and an F1-score of 68%, while Random Forest followed with 80% accuracy. The results demonstrate that fusing shallow and deep features with traditional machine learning models can effectively handle data imbalance and provide competitive classification accuracy for skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Binary label: malignant vs. benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin lesions into malignant and benign categories using the HAM10000 dataset. Features (position-wise color, SIFT, and MobileNetV3 deep features) were fused and fed into five traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes) to predict discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset introduced by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), consisting of 10,015 dermatoscopic images collected over 20 years from the Medical University of Vienna and the personal practice of Cliff Rosendahl. Images have a resolution of 600x450 pixels.

use.training: Used for training and parameter tuning of five traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes) after feature extraction and oversampling/balancing.
use.other: The dataset was grouped into two binary classes: malignant (MEL, BCC, AKIEC) and benign (BKL, NV, VASC, DF) for binary classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To oversample the minority class (malignant lesions) in the training set to balance the data distribution and address class imbalance.
To divide the HAM10000 dataset into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets for model evaluation.
To maximize the performance of the five machine learning classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes).
To group SIFT visual words into a dictionary of features for feature extraction.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall metrics on a held-out test set.

preprocessing: RGB to grayscale conversion (in one occasion)
HAM10000.notes: The SVM model achieved the highest accuracy (81%). The paper claims a 1% improvement over several state-of-the-art researches and outperforms deep learning-based methods in their comparison. The study highlights that fusing shallow and deep features with traditional machine learning models effectively handles data imbalance.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV3 (used as a feature extractor via transfer learning) combined with traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes)
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV3 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: 600x450 pixels (original dataset resolution); images were resized during preprocessing.

architecture_details.feature_extractor: MobileNetV3 convolutional neural network architecture used to extract deep features.
shallow_features: Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) features (dimension 128 per feature, clustered via K-means)
architecture_details.fusion: Shallow and deep features were fused to form a single representative feature vector for each image.

training_details.platform: Google Colab with Nvidia K80 GPU
preprocessing: RGB to grayscale conversion (in one occasion)
training_details.imbalance_handling: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) used on the training set to balance malignant and benign classes.
training_details.optimization: Parameter tuning was performed on the five traditional machine learning classifiers to maximize performance.

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filter application for quality enhancement
RGB to grayscale color space conversion (in one occasion)
Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) for handling class imbalance in the training set

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to oversample the minority class (malignant lesions) in the training set to balance the data distribution and address class imbalance. SMOTE creates synthetic data points utilizing the characteristics of existing samples rather than generating duplicates.

conclusions: Fusing shallow features (position-wise color and SIFT) with deep features extracted via the MobileNetV3 transfer learning model effectively improves the classification of malignant versus benign skin lesions.

Traditional machine learning classifiers, particularly the Support Vector Machine (SVM), can achieve competitive performance (81% accuracy, 68% F1-score) when provided with fused feature sets, offering a time-optimized alternative to deep learning methods.
The proposed method successfully addresses the challenge of class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, where malignant samples are scarce, by utilizing feature fusion and parameter tuning.
The SVM model outperformed other tested traditional machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, KNN, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes) in terms of accuracy.
The study demonstrates that image processing and traditional machine learning models are of significant value for skin lesion classification when provided with well-formed, fused features.
The proposed method shows a 1% improvement over several state-of-the-art researches and outperforms deep learning-based methods in the authors' comparison.

Limitations and future work include the need to validate the method on other datasets and explore additional feature extraction techniques such as ABCD features and various convolutional neural network architectures.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (NV class comprises ~60% of samples) and demographic bias (collected from Vienna and one private practice), limiting generalizability.
No external validation was performed; the authors explicitly state that validating on other datasets is a future scope, raising concerns about the robustness of the 81% accuracy claim.

The binary classification task groups Actinic Keratosis and Intraepithelial Carcinoma (AKIEC) into the malignant class, despite the paper noting AKIEC is potentially benign, which may introduce diagnostic ambiguity.

The reported accuracy (81%) is modest for skin lesion classification tasks and significantly lower than many state-of-the-art deep learning models (e.g., 90-96% cited in related work), suggesting limited clinical utility compared to current standards.

The paper claims a 1% improvement over 'several state-of-the-art researches' but compares against older or less rigorous baselines (e.g., 76-80% accuracy) rather than recent high-performing deep learning benchmarks, potentially overstating the contribution.
Data leakage risk exists due to the use of SMOTE on the training set without explicit confirmation that no test data influenced the synthetic sample generation, although an 80-20 split was used.

The methodology uses traditional machine learning classifiers (SVM, RF, etc.) with MobileNetV3 as a feature extractor rather than end-to-end training, which may limit the model's ability to learn optimal feature representations for this specific domain.
The study does not report confidence intervals, p-values, or statistical significance tests for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the reliability of the results.
The dataset includes images collected over 20 years from different sources, which may introduce heterogeneity in image quality and acquisition protocols not addressed in the preprocessing steps.
